Getting started with typethis

Source: vignettes/getting-started.Rmd
getting-started.Rmd

typethis adds runtime type safety to R. This vignette is a 10-minute tour of the four things you’ll reach for most: type checks, typed functions, typed models, and composable type specs.

library(typethis)
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'typethis'
#> The following object is masked from 'package:methods':
#> 
#>     signature

1. Type checks

The lowest-level entry points test, assert, and validate types. Use them at function boundaries, when parsing inputs, or anywhere a quick sanity check is enough.

is_type(5, "numeric")
#> [1] TRUE
is_type("hello", "numeric")
#> [1] FALSE

# assert_type() throws on mismatch
assert_type(5, "numeric", "x")

# validate_type() returns a list instead of throwing
validate_type("hello", "numeric", "x")
#> $valid
#> [1] FALSE
#> 
#> $error
#> [1] "Type error: 'x' must be numeric, got character"

is_type() accepts any of:

  • a builtin name — "numeric", "integer", "character", "logical", "list", "data.frame", "matrix", "factor", "date", "posixct", "function", "environment";
  • a predicate function function(value) -> logical;
  • a type spec such as t_union("integer", "character").

For safe coercion, use coerce_type():

coerce_type("123", "numeric")
#> [1] 123
coerce_type(c(1, 0, 1), "logical")
#> [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE

2. Typed functions

typed_function() wraps a function so each call is validated.

greet <- typed_function(
  function(name, times = 1L) paste(rep(name, times), collapse = " "),
  arg_specs = list(name = "character", times = "integer"),
  return_spec = "character"
)

greet("hi", times = 3L)
#> [1] "hi hi hi"

A bad call fails before the body runs:

greet("hi", times = "three")
#> Error:
#> ! Type error: 'times' must be integer, got character

typed_function() supports every R calling convention — positional, named, mixed, reordered, and ... passthrough — and respects argument defaults. Set coerce = TRUE to convert mismatches before checking, or validate = FALSE for hot paths where you’ve already validated upstream.

For the full feature set see ?typed_function and the validators-and-models vignette.

Retrofitting existing functions

When you want to add type checks to a function you already have, as_typed() removes the boilerplate of restating every argument. Specs for arguments with literal atomic defaults are inferred automatically; only arguments you want to override appear in the call.

add <- as_typed(function(x = 0L, y = 0L) x + y, .return = "integer")
add(2L, 3L)
#> [1] 5
add("two", 3L)
#> Error:
#> ! Type error: 'x' must be integer, got character

Pass overrides by name. Inference fills in the rest:

greet <- as_typed(
  function(name = "world", times = 1L) {
    paste(rep(name, times), collapse = " ")
  },
  name = t_vector_of("character", exact_length = 1L)
)
greet("hi", times = 3L)
#> [1] "hi hi hi"

Pass name = NULL to opt one argument out of validation, or .infer = FALSE to disable inference entirely. See ?as_typed and ?infer_specs for the full inference rules.

For an existing typed function, types() is a symmetric replacement-form accessor over as_typed():

greet_clone <- function(name = "world", times = 1L) {
  paste(rep(name, times), collapse = " ")
}
types(greet_clone) <- types(greet)
is_typed(greet_clone)
#> [1] TRUE

# NULL un-types
types(greet_clone) <- NULL
is_typed(greet_clone)
#> [1] FALSE

To retrofit a whole environment in one call, as_typed_env() walks the environment, applies as_typed() to every function it finds, and writes the typed versions back. Per-function overrides flow through .specs:

e <- new.env()
e$add <- function(x = 0L, y = 0L) x + y
e$greet <- function(name = "world") paste0("hi ", name)

as_typed_env(e, .specs = list(
  add = list(.return = "integer")
))
is_typed(e$add)
#> [1] TRUE
attr(e$add, "return_spec")
#> [1] "integer"

3. Typed models

Use define_model() to describe a record type — a configuration object, an API request body, a domain entity. It generates a new_<Class>() constructor and an update_<Class>() updater in the calling environment.

define_model("User", fields = list(
  name = field("character"),
  age = field("integer",
    validator = numeric_range(0, 120),
    default = 0L
  ),
  email = field("character",
    validator = string_pattern("^[^@]+@[^@]+\\.[^@]+$")
  )
))

ada <- new_User(name = "Ada Lovelace", email = "ada@example.com")
ada
#> <Typed Model: User>
#> Fields:
#>   name: character = Ada Lovelace
#>   email: character = ada@example.com
#>   age: integer = 0

Validation runs at construction time:

new_User(name = "Ada", age = 200L, email = "ada@example.com")
#> Error:
#> ! Validation failed for field 'age' in User

update_User() returns a fresh instance with the named fields replaced and revalidated:

ada <- update_User(ada, age = 36L)
ada$age
#> [1] 36

Models nest. Reference one model from another by class name:

define_model("Address", fields = list(
  street = field("character"),
  city   = field("character")
))

define_model("Person", fields = list(
  name    = field("character"),
  address = field("Address")
))

p <- new_Person(
  name    = "Ada",
  address = new_Address(street = "Newstead Abbey", city = "Nottingham")
)
p$address$city
#> [1] "Nottingham"

For defaults, nullability, strict mode, and validators, see the validators-and-models vignette.

4. Composable type specs

The t_*() family builds richer specifications that work everywhere a plain type name does (is_type(), assert_type(), field(), typed_function(), to_json_schema()).

id <- t_union("integer", "character")
is_type(1L, id)
#> [1] TRUE
is_type("u-42", id)
#> [1] TRUE

maybe_int <- t_nullable("integer")
is_type(NULL, maybe_int)
#> [1] TRUE

tags <- t_list_of("character", min_length = 1L)
is_type(list("alpha", "beta"), tags)
#> [1] TRUE

role <- t_enum(c("admin", "user", "guest"))
is_type("admin", role)
#> [1] TRUE

Specs compose:

mixed <- t_list_of(t_union("integer", "character"))
is_type(list(1L, "two", 3L), mixed)
#> [1] TRUE

Use any spec inside a field() or as typed_function()’s arg_specs entry. See the type-specs vignette for the full reference.

Where to go next

  • validators-and-models — built-in validators, defaults, nullability, strict mode, the field() metadata.
  • type-specs — every t_*() constructor and how they compose.
  • interop — JSON Schema, Open Data Contract Standard, and OpenAPI 3.1 export and import.

For function reference, ?typethis is a topic-grouped index and each function’s help page has a “See also” section linking to its family.
